title: Nonadiabatic Renormalization Group for Strongly Coupled Multiscale Quantum Systems
abstract: Complex quantum systems are often multiscale in nature with strong interactions between different scales. We present a novel idea: iteratively suppressing, rather than tracing out, the fast, high-energy degrees of freedom in strongly correlated quantum systems with multiple energy scales in a non-perturbative way, termed nonadiabatic renormalization group. This leads to a quantum geometric structure of a nested fiber bundle, in which each fiber of a layer is itself a fiber bundle of the next layer. The nonadiabatic renormalization group brings a new type of tensor network states that shares physical legs among ''sites'' and encodes quantum entanglement beyond conventional matrix product states. We demonstrate how to apply the nonadiabatic renormalization group to different types of problems, including an interacting boson model and ab initio quantum chemistry with interacting electrons.
summary: The paper proposes a new renormalization group method called NARG to treat quantum systems with hierarchical energy scales. It introduces a new tensor network, LETTA, which shares physical legs between sites to better encode nonadiabatic entanglement. The method proves highly effective for both interacting boson models and molecular electronic correlation problems.
main_problem: Efficiently modeling complex, multiscale quantum systems where different energy or time scales are strongly coupled, particularly when standard 'tracing out' or product basis approaches fail in the strong-coupling regime.
main_result: The introduction of the Nonadiabatic Renormalization Group (NARG) and the Leg-Tied Tensor Ansatz (LETTA), a new tensor network state that captures entanglement beyond conventional MPS by sharing physical legs among sites.
method: An iterative approach that suppresses high-energy degrees of freedom to construct a nested fiber bundle structure, resulting in a tensor network (LETTA) where physical legs are shared across scales.
model_or_system: The method is demonstrated on an interacting boson model with strong anharmonicity and ab initio quantum chemistry problems, specifically the hydrogen chain (H8) for electronic correlation.
key_observables: Low-lying many-body eigenstates, correlation energy, global overlap matrix, and the quantum geometric tensor (Riemann metric and Berry curvature).
important_parameters: Energy scales (Lambda vs. mu), bond dimension (number of retained adiabatic states D), and coupling strengths (anharmonicity and mode-coupling).
main_assumptions: The method assumes a hierarchy of energy scales and relies on the truncation of adiabatic eigenstates (coarse-graining) to manage the Hilbert space.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares MPS to the new LETTA architecture; Figure 2 illustrates the NARG as a nested fiber bundle; Figure 3 shows eigenvalue convergence in a boson model; Figure 4 compares ground state energy accuracy against HF and FCI methods.
paper_structure: The paper begins by identifying the failure of standard methods in strong coupling, introduces the NARG framework and its geometric fiber bundle structure, derives the LETTA tensor network, and concludes with numerical demonstrations on boson and electronic systems.
why_interesting: It provides a novel tensor network architecture (LETTA) specifically designed for multiscale dynamics, offering a way to handle nonadiabaticity and strong coupling that standard MPS-based methods struggle to capture.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Schematic of NARG as a nested fiber bundle whereby the fiber of each layer is itself a fiber bundle.

FIG. 1. Schematic of the leg-tied tensor ansatz (LETTA) in comparison with the conventional matrix product states (MPS). Physical legs can be shared between (a) nearest neigh- bours and (b) next-nearest-neighbours. (d) A LETTA with peridic boundary condition.

FIG. 3. Low-lying eigenstates of an strongly interacting bo- son model with 20 modes. The lowerst 16 eigenvalues are shown, with a rapid convergence with respect to the number of retained adiabatic states D.

FIG. 4. NARG for ab initio quantum chemistry. Electronic correlation is gradually incorporated into the NARG by in- creasing the retained adiabatic states. More than 80% of the correlation energy in the hydrogen chain H8 is captured by D = 200.
